- # Record, replay, and review: UI automation with Xcode



Learn to record, run, and maintain XCUIAutomation tests in Xcode. Replay your XCTest UI tests in dozens of locales, device types, and system conditions using test plan configurations. Review your test results using the Xcode test report, and download screenshots and videos of your runs. We'll also cover best practices for preparing your app for automation with Accessibility and writing stable, high-quality automation code.

0:06







[Hey, I’m Max, and I’m an engineer on the Xcode team.](https://developer.apple.com/videos/play/wwdc2025/344/?time=6) [There are so many awesome features and experiences inside of Xcode,](https://developer.apple.com/videos/play/wwdc2025/344/?time=10) [it can be overwhelming.](https://developer.apple.com/videos/play/wwdc2025/344/?time=14) [For example, did you know it’s possible to watch your app run on dozens of devices,](https://developer.apple.com/videos/play/wwdc2025/344/?time=15) [languages, and configurations with one click?](https://developer.apple.com/videos/play/wwdc2025/344/?time=20) [And on top of that,](https://developer.apple.com/videos/play/wwdc2025/344/?time=23) [you can get a full quality video recording of every single run.](https://developer.apple.com/videos/play/wwdc2025/344/?time=24) [Well, that’s totally possible with the power of UI automation in Xcode.](https://developer.apple.com/videos/play/wwdc2025/344/?time=27) [Let's take a look at how that works.](https://developer.apple.com/videos/play/wwdc2025/344/?time=31) [First, we’ll cover an overview of UI automation.](https://developer.apple.com/videos/play/wwdc2025/344/?time=33) [Then we’ll prepare your app, record your interactions as automation code,](https://developer.apple.com/videos/play/wwdc2025/344/?time=37) [replay the automation across several devices and languages,](https://developer.apple.com/videos/play/wwdc2025/344/?time=43) [and finally, watch video recordings of the results](https://developer.apple.com/videos/play/wwdc2025/344/?time=46) [and see a report about whether each run passed or failed.](https://developer.apple.com/videos/play/wwdc2025/344/?time=49)







0:54







[Let’s start with an overview of how UI automation works.](https://developer.apple.com/videos/play/wwdc2025/344/?time=54)







0:58







[Inside Xcode, we have two testing frameworks: Swift Testing and XCTest.](https://developer.apple.com/videos/play/wwdc2025/344/?time=58) [Both frameworks are capable of quickly testing your app](https://developer.apple.com/videos/play/wwdc2025/344/?time=63) [and your source code in many configurations.](https://developer.apple.com/videos/play/wwdc2025/344/?time=66)







1:10







[When you import XCTest,](https://developer.apple.com/videos/play/wwdc2025/344/?time=70) [a framework called XCUIAutomation is automatically included.](https://developer.apple.com/videos/play/wwdc2025/344/?time=72) [XCUIAutomation can be used to automate your app](https://developer.apple.com/videos/play/wwdc2025/344/?time=76) [and interact with it like a person does.](https://developer.apple.com/videos/play/wwdc2025/344/?time=79) [These frameworks work together to provide a complete app testing suite,](https://developer.apple.com/videos/play/wwdc2025/344/?time=81) [and if you ask me, I think that’s pretty sweet.](https://developer.apple.com/videos/play/wwdc2025/344/?time=85)







1:29







[A complete app testing suite is usually comprised of both unit](https://developer.apple.com/videos/play/wwdc2025/344/?time=89) [and UI automation tests.](https://developer.apple.com/videos/play/wwdc2025/344/?time=92) [Unit tests test your app’s logic and models, and with Swift testing,](https://developer.apple.com/videos/play/wwdc2025/344/?time=94) [it’s possible to run tests on frameworks](https://developer.apple.com/videos/play/wwdc2025/344/?time=98) [and Swift packages that don’t even have a user interface.](https://developer.apple.com/videos/play/wwdc2025/344/?time=101)







1:45







[Meanwhile, UI automation tests validate your app’s user experience,](https://developer.apple.com/videos/play/wwdc2025/344/?time=105) [as well as its integration with Apple hardware](https://developer.apple.com/videos/play/wwdc2025/344/?time=109) [and the behavior of common workflows.](https://developer.apple.com/videos/play/wwdc2025/344/?time=112)







1:55







[Generally, your test suite will have more unit tests than UI tests,](https://developer.apple.com/videos/play/wwdc2025/344/?time=115) [and you want to aim for full coverage of your code.](https://developer.apple.com/videos/play/wwdc2025/344/?time=119) [But UI automation tests let you see how your app looks,](https://developer.apple.com/videos/play/wwdc2025/344/?time=122) [behaves, and integrates with the rest of the operating system.](https://developer.apple.com/videos/play/wwdc2025/344/?time=125) [There are so many benefits to doing this.](https://developer.apple.com/videos/play/wwdc2025/344/?time=129)







2:12







[For example, you can test your app as a person would with gestures like taps,](https://developer.apple.com/videos/play/wwdc2025/344/?time=132) [swipes and clicks.](https://developer.apple.com/videos/play/wwdc2025/344/?time=136) [You can understand how your app is perceived](https://developer.apple.com/videos/play/wwdc2025/344/?time=138) [by people who use assistive technologies](https://developer.apple.com/videos/play/wwdc2025/344/?time=140) [like VoiceOver, Voice Control, and Dynamic Type.](https://developer.apple.com/videos/play/wwdc2025/344/?time=142)







2:26







[You can view your app running](https://developer.apple.com/videos/play/wwdc2025/344/?time=146) [on all of the languages and regions that it supports](https://developer.apple.com/videos/play/wwdc2025/344/?time=148) [and focus on languages that have large effects on the look of your app,](https://developer.apple.com/videos/play/wwdc2025/344/?time=150) [like languages with longer strings and languages with a right-to-left layout.](https://developer.apple.com/videos/play/wwdc2025/344/?time=154) [You can test your app’s integration with Apple hardware features,](https://developer.apple.com/videos/play/wwdc2025/344/?time=159) [like the Action button, camera button,](https://developer.apple.com/videos/play/wwdc2025/344/?time=162) [Apple TV remote, and Apple Watch’s Digital Crown.](https://developer.apple.com/videos/play/wwdc2025/344/?time=164) [And finally, you can test your app’s launch performance,](https://developer.apple.com/videos/play/wwdc2025/344/?time=168) [which is a key metric in understanding how quickly people can get started](https://developer.apple.com/videos/play/wwdc2025/344/?time=171) [using your app.](https://developer.apple.com/videos/play/wwdc2025/344/?time=174) [To set up a UI automation workflow, there are three key phases:](https://developer.apple.com/videos/play/wwdc2025/344/?time=176) [record, replay, and review.](https://developer.apple.com/videos/play/wwdc2025/344/?time=180)







3:03







[First, record your interactions like taps, swipes, and hardware button presses,](https://developer.apple.com/videos/play/wwdc2025/344/?time=183) [then watch Xcode automatically write those as code.](https://developer.apple.com/videos/play/wwdc2025/344/?time=189) [Then, replay your interactions across multiple devices,](https://developer.apple.com/videos/play/wwdc2025/344/?time=192) [languages, regions, and device orientations,](https://developer.apple.com/videos/play/wwdc2025/344/?time=196) [both on your devices and in Xcode Cloud.](https://developer.apple.com/videos/play/wwdc2025/344/?time=199) [Finally, review videos and results of your app running](https://developer.apple.com/videos/play/wwdc2025/344/?time=202) [in all of those configurations and see which ones passed](https://developer.apple.com/videos/play/wwdc2025/344/?time=206) [and which ones failed.](https://developer.apple.com/videos/play/wwdc2025/344/?time=210)







3:32







[UI automation is supported on all Apple platforms:](https://developer.apple.com/videos/play/wwdc2025/344/?time=212) [iOS, iPadOS, macOS, watchOS, tvOS, and visionOS (Designed for iPad).](https://developer.apple.com/videos/play/wwdc2025/344/?time=215) [The same automation can even run on multiple platforms.](https://developer.apple.com/videos/play/wwdc2025/344/?time=222) [So it’s possible to build an automation once](https://developer.apple.com/videos/play/wwdc2025/344/?time=225) [and run it on all your supported devices.](https://developer.apple.com/videos/play/wwdc2025/344/?time=228) [That means you can see how your app runs on Mac, iPhone and Vision Pro](https://developer.apple.com/videos/play/wwdc2025/344/?time=230) [with one click and no code changes.](https://developer.apple.com/videos/play/wwdc2025/344/?time=235) [Let’s briefly go over how UI automation works.](https://developer.apple.com/videos/play/wwdc2025/344/?time=238)







4:01







[UI automation interacts with your app as a person does](https://developer.apple.com/videos/play/wwdc2025/344/?time=241) [using gestures and hardware events.](https://developer.apple.com/videos/play/wwdc2025/344/?time=244) [Automation runs completely independently from your app,](https://developer.apple.com/videos/play/wwdc2025/344/?time=247) [so your app models and data are not directly accessible.](https://developer.apple.com/videos/play/wwdc2025/344/?time=250)







4:15







[UI automation tells the operating system which gestures to perform,](https://developer.apple.com/videos/play/wwdc2025/344/?time=255) [then synchronously waits for them to complete one at a time.](https://developer.apple.com/videos/play/wwdc2025/344/?time=259)







4:24







[These actions include launching your app, interacting with buttons and navigation,](https://developer.apple.com/videos/play/wwdc2025/344/?time=264) [setting system state like Dark Mode,](https://developer.apple.com/videos/play/wwdc2025/344/?time=268) [and even setting a simulated location for the device if you wanted to.](https://developer.apple.com/videos/play/wwdc2025/344/?time=270) [At Apple, accessibility is one of our core values.](https://developer.apple.com/videos/play/wwdc2025/344/?time=275) [Apple’s assistive technologies make sure that everyone can use your app](https://developer.apple.com/videos/play/wwdc2025/344/?time=278) [regardless of any physical, visual, audible or motor impairment.](https://developer.apple.com/videos/play/wwdc2025/344/?time=282) [We work really hard to ensure that most of these technologies](https://developer.apple.com/videos/play/wwdc2025/344/?time=287) [work with your app by default,](https://developer.apple.com/videos/play/wwdc2025/344/?time=290) [with no work needed from you as a developer.](https://developer.apple.com/videos/play/wwdc2025/344/?time=292) [That said, adding additional support can enrich your app’s experience](https://developer.apple.com/videos/play/wwdc2025/344/?time=294) [and make it even easier to automate.](https://developer.apple.com/videos/play/wwdc2025/344/?time=299) [The thing is, accessibility is the underlying framework](https://developer.apple.com/videos/play/wwdc2025/344/?time=302) [that powers UI automation.](https://developer.apple.com/videos/play/wwdc2025/344/?time=305) [Having a great accessibility experience](https://developer.apple.com/videos/play/wwdc2025/344/?time=306) [means you get a great UI automation experience too.](https://developer.apple.com/videos/play/wwdc2025/344/?time=309)







5:12







[Accessibility provides information directly to UI automation,](https://developer.apple.com/videos/play/wwdc2025/344/?time=312) [such as element types, labels, values and frames](https://developer.apple.com/videos/play/wwdc2025/344/?time=315) [but what accessibility sees](https://developer.apple.com/videos/play/wwdc2025/344/?time=320) [is not necessarily one-to-one with what you see as a person.](https://developer.apple.com/videos/play/wwdc2025/344/?time=321)







5:26







[Let’s take a look at an example.](https://developer.apple.com/videos/play/wwdc2025/344/?time=326) [On this screen, the Great Barrier Reef button is visible in the UI](https://developer.apple.com/videos/play/wwdc2025/344/?time=328) [but accessibility can see even more than that.](https://developer.apple.com/videos/play/wwdc2025/344/?time=333) [Sure, accessibility can see the elements type and label](https://developer.apple.com/videos/play/wwdc2025/344/?time=336) [and expose them to UI automation](https://developer.apple.com/videos/play/wwdc2025/344/?time=339) [but the identifier property is also exposed.](https://developer.apple.com/videos/play/wwdc2025/344/?time=341) [The accessibility identifier can be used to describe any element on screen uniquely,](https://developer.apple.com/videos/play/wwdc2025/344/?time=345) [relative to all the elements around it.](https://developer.apple.com/videos/play/wwdc2025/344/?time=349) [The identifier is not designed to be localized,](https://developer.apple.com/videos/play/wwdc2025/344/?time=352) [so you can use it to refer to the same UI element in any language or device.](https://developer.apple.com/videos/play/wwdc2025/344/?time=355)







6:00







[For checkboxes and other stateful elements,](https://developer.apple.com/videos/play/wwdc2025/344/?time=360) [a value property can be used.](https://developer.apple.com/videos/play/wwdc2025/344/?time=363) [This reveals the element’s current state to both accessibility and UI automation.](https://developer.apple.com/videos/play/wwdc2025/344/?time=365)







6:11







[To learn way more about accessibility,](https://developer.apple.com/videos/play/wwdc2025/344/?time=371) [check out “Build accessible apps with SwiftUI and UIKit” from WWDC23,](https://developer.apple.com/videos/play/wwdc2025/344/?time=374) [or “SwiftUI Accessibility: Beyond the basics” from WWDC21.](https://developer.apple.com/videos/play/wwdc2025/344/?time=380)







6:25







[Now that we’ve explored how a UI automation works with accessibility,](https://developer.apple.com/videos/play/wwdc2025/344/?time=385) [let’s prepare your app automation.](https://developer.apple.com/videos/play/wwdc2025/344/?time=389)







6:32







[First, we’ll add accessibility identifiers.](https://developer.apple.com/videos/play/wwdc2025/344/?time=392) [Then we’ll do a quick review of your app’s accessibility.](https://developer.apple.com/videos/play/wwdc2025/344/?time=395) [And finally, we’ll add a new UI testing target](https://developer.apple.com/videos/play/wwdc2025/344/?time=399) [to get ready to record our interactions as code.](https://developer.apple.com/videos/play/wwdc2025/344/?time=402) [Before we dive in, it's worth noting](https://developer.apple.com/videos/play/wwdc2025/344/?time=405) [that your app already fully supports UI automation and UI recording out of the box](https://developer.apple.com/videos/play/wwdc2025/344/?time=407) [with no work needed from you as a developer.](https://developer.apple.com/videos/play/wwdc2025/344/?time=412) [The steps we’re about to cover are not required](https://developer.apple.com/videos/play/wwdc2025/344/?time=415) [but they can lead to better and higher-quality results.](https://developer.apple.com/videos/play/wwdc2025/344/?time=418) [Adding accessibility identifiers can be done in your view code](https://developer.apple.com/videos/play/wwdc2025/344/?time=422) [written in SwiftUI, UIKit or AppKit.](https://developer.apple.com/videos/play/wwdc2025/344/?time=425) [Accessibility identifiers are the best way to uniquely identify](https://developer.apple.com/videos/play/wwdc2025/344/?time=428) [any element in your app for automation.](https://developer.apple.com/videos/play/wwdc2025/344/?time=432) [It’s a good idea to add accessibility identifiers](https://developer.apple.com/videos/play/wwdc2025/344/?time=435) [to elements with localized strings or dynamic content.](https://developer.apple.com/videos/play/wwdc2025/344/?time=438) [That includes content found in any of your data models](https://developer.apple.com/videos/play/wwdc2025/344/?time=441) [or content that’s downloaded from the internet.](https://developer.apple.com/videos/play/wwdc2025/344/?time=444)







7:27







[Good identifiers are unique within your entire app,](https://developer.apple.com/videos/play/wwdc2025/344/?time=447) [descriptive enough to detail the element that they are on,](https://developer.apple.com/videos/play/wwdc2025/344/?time=451) [and static, not reacting to changes in content.](https://developer.apple.com/videos/play/wwdc2025/344/?time=455)







7:39







[While titles and descriptions might change, good identifiers always describe](https://developer.apple.com/videos/play/wwdc2025/344/?time=459) [the contents of the element they are attached to.](https://developer.apple.com/videos/play/wwdc2025/344/?time=463) [That way, we can be sure my lovely landmarks stick around forever.](https://developer.apple.com/videos/play/wwdc2025/344/?time=466)







7:52







[In SwiftUI, you can add the accessibilityIdentifier modifier](https://developer.apple.com/videos/play/wwdc2025/344/?time=472) [to any UI element.](https://developer.apple.com/videos/play/wwdc2025/344/?time=476) [It will be recognized as long as your view](https://developer.apple.com/videos/play/wwdc2025/344/?time=478) [and its parent views are not hidden from accessibility.](https://developer.apple.com/videos/play/wwdc2025/344/?time=480)







8:04







[It’s a good idea to make identifiers specific to an instance of a view,](https://developer.apple.com/videos/play/wwdc2025/344/?time=484) [especially for views that are used many times in your app.](https://developer.apple.com/videos/play/wwdc2025/344/?time=488) [In this example, we use the landmark’s id property](https://developer.apple.com/videos/play/wwdc2025/344/?time=492) [to make the identifier unique for each one.](https://developer.apple.com/videos/play/wwdc2025/344/?time=495)







8:19







[In UIKit, the accessibilityIdentifier property](https://developer.apple.com/videos/play/wwdc2025/344/?time=499) [can be set on any UIView as long as the view is an accessibility element.](https://developer.apple.com/videos/play/wwdc2025/344/?time=502) [Most UI views, like controls, text and images,](https://developer.apple.com/videos/play/wwdc2025/344/?time=507) [are accessibility elements by default](https://developer.apple.com/videos/play/wwdc2025/344/?time=510) [so usually no work is needed to do this.](https://developer.apple.com/videos/play/wwdc2025/344/?time=513)







8:36







[Properties like accessibilityLabel, accessibilityTraits](https://developer.apple.com/videos/play/wwdc2025/344/?time=516) [and accessibilityValue are useful to assistive technologies like VoiceOver](https://developer.apple.com/videos/play/wwdc2025/344/?time=520) [and are also useful to UI automation.](https://developer.apple.com/videos/play/wwdc2025/344/?time=524)







8:48







[However, the accessibilityIdentifier property](https://developer.apple.com/videos/play/wwdc2025/344/?time=528) [is not read out loud by VoiceOver](https://developer.apple.com/videos/play/wwdc2025/344/?time=531) [and is not exposed to anyone using your app.](https://developer.apple.com/videos/play/wwdc2025/344/?time=533) [This makes it a useful way to provide information](https://developer.apple.com/videos/play/wwdc2025/344/?time=536) [that is only useful to automation,](https://developer.apple.com/videos/play/wwdc2025/344/?time=539) [like the index of a table cell or the symbol name of an image.](https://developer.apple.com/videos/play/wwdc2025/344/?time=541) [I can even have the coding assistant in Xcode](https://developer.apple.com/videos/play/wwdc2025/344/?time=545) [add accessibility identifiers for me.](https://developer.apple.com/videos/play/wwdc2025/344/?time=548) [For example, I can write,](https://developer.apple.com/videos/play/wwdc2025/344/?time=550) [“Add accessibility identifiers to the relevant parts of this view”,](https://developer.apple.com/videos/play/wwdc2025/344/?time=552) [and it will just happen.](https://developer.apple.com/videos/play/wwdc2025/344/?time=556) [The coding assistant even knows how to use the id property on a landmark](https://developer.apple.com/videos/play/wwdc2025/344/?time=557) [to make each identifier completely unique.](https://developer.apple.com/videos/play/wwdc2025/344/?time=561) [Pretty cool.](https://developer.apple.com/videos/play/wwdc2025/344/?time=564) [Reviewing your app’s overall accessibility behavior](https://developer.apple.com/videos/play/wwdc2025/344/?time=566) [is a good idea before starting UI recording.](https://developer.apple.com/videos/play/wwdc2025/344/?time=568) [It’s like flossing before going to the dentist.](https://developer.apple.com/videos/play/wwdc2025/344/?time=571) [You’re going to know exactly how well you’ve been doing soon.](https://developer.apple.com/videos/play/wwdc2025/344/?time=573) [This will just give you a good preview of it.](https://developer.apple.com/videos/play/wwdc2025/344/?time=575) [Xcode ships with an app called Accessibility Inspector,](https://developer.apple.com/videos/play/wwdc2025/344/?time=578) [which lets you find, diagnose and fix accessibility issues.](https://developer.apple.com/videos/play/wwdc2025/344/?time=581) [The Inspector can be launched from the Xcode top-level menu](https://developer.apple.com/videos/play/wwdc2025/344/?time=585) [under Open Developer Tool.](https://developer.apple.com/videos/play/wwdc2025/344/?time=588) [It can also be launched from Spotlight.](https://developer.apple.com/videos/play/wwdc2025/344/?time=591)







9:54

        app.cells["Landmark-186"].tap()
        XCTAssertTrue(

- - 0:00 - [Introduction and agenda](https://developer.apple.com/videos/play/wwdc2025/344/?time=0)
  - Xcode's UI automation, powered by the XCUIAutomation framework, enables you to test your apps comprehensively. It works alongside Swift Testing and the XCTest framework, providing a complete app-testing suite. UI automation tests validate an app's user experience, its integration with Apple hardware, and the behavior of common workflows, complementing unit tests which focus on app logic and data models.

    The process involves three main phases:
    Recording interactions, which are then automatically converted into code.
    Replaying these interactions across various devices, languages, regions, and orientations.
    Reviewing video recordings and results of each UI test run.

    These automations allow you to test your apps as real users would, ensuring accessibility, language support, and hardware integration. It is supported on all Apple platforms, enabling you to build and run automation tests once for multiple devices, such as iPhone, iPad, Mac, Apple TV, and Apple Watch, with a single click.

  - 3:58 - [UI automation overview](https://developer.apple.com/videos/play/wwdc2025/344/?time=238)
  - UI automation mimics user interactions with an app, launching it, tapping buttons, and navigating through screens. It relies on the app's accessibility features — which provide labels, values, and unique identifiers for elements — to perform these actions independently. Accessibility, a core Apple value, ensures apps are usable by everyone and also powers UI automation.

  - 6:26 - [Prepare your app for automation](https://developer.apple.com/videos/play/wwdc2025/344/?time=386)
  - To prepare the app automation, several steps are involved. First, add accessibility identifiers to the app's elements. These identifiers are unique labels written in SwiftUI, UIKit, or AppKit that help automation tools recognize and interact with specific parts of the app. They are particularly useful for elements with localized strings or dynamic content and need to be descriptive, static, and unique within the entire app.

    Next, conduct a quick review of the app's accessibility using Accessibility Inspector. This tool helps identify and fix any accessibility issues, ensuring that the app is well-prepared for automation. Accessibility Inspector allows you to view and modify accessibility properties for each view, making the app more usable for both assistive technologies and automation scripts.

    Finally, add a new UI testing target to the Xcode project. This target provides a dedicated space for storing the automation code. Once the target is set up, you can begin recording your interactions with the app, which will be automatically translated into Swift code, streamlining the automation process.

  - 11:32 - [Record your interactions](https://developer.apple.com/videos/play/wwdc2025/344/?time=692)
  - The Landmarks sample project, shown in Xcode and Simulator, demonstrates planning a virtual vacation to Australia. UI recordings capture interactions within the app as code. The example adds a new collection, renames it "Max’s Australian Adventure," and populates it with landmarks like the Great Barrier Reef and Uluru.

    After the recording stops, the example reviews the generated code, selects appropriate UI queries, and adds validations using XCTest to ensure the app's functionality. The test is then rerun, successfully verifying the creation of the collection and the addition of landmarks. You can further enhance the tests by setting up specific device states, simulating locations, and performing accessibility audits.

  - 17:30 - [Replay in multiple configurations](https://developer.apple.com/videos/play/wwdc2025/344/?time=1050)
  - Xcode Cloud enables automated app testing in various configurations at-desk and in the cloud, as well as for different languages and devices. Test plans organize tests, set system settings, and manage properties. Configurations allow testing in specific locales, including languages with longer strings or right-to-left scripts.

    Xcode Cloud runs tests in the cloud, saving videos and screenshots of failures for review. An entire team can view run history, logs, and results, facilitating collaboration and remote monitoring. Advanced configurations and further details are available in Developer Documentation.

  - 20:54 - [Review videos and results](https://developer.apple.com/videos/play/wwdc2025/344/?time=1254)
  - The Xcode test report provides a comprehensive tool for analyzing test results. Upon identifying a failed test run, you can access video recordings and detailed descriptions. The report overlays UI interactions on the video and includes a timeline with failure diamonds for quick navigation.

    You can view present UI elements at the failure point, receive code suggestions, and directly edit your test code. After making corrections, the test can be rerun, and the report allows you to verify functionality across different languages and layouts, ultimately enhancing app quality and accessibility.

  - 24:16 - [Next steps](https://developer.apple.com/videos/play/wwdc2025/344/?time=1456)
  - For more information on unit testing and Swift testing, see "Meet Swift Testing" session from WWDC24; for questions or feedback, visit the Developer Forums.

# Understanding XCUITest Framework: Your Guide to Efficient iOS Testing

[![Pcloudy - AI Testing Platform](https://miro.medium.com/v2/resize:fill:32:32/1*vDBJG7iWfzWoXvkysJYwXA.jpeg)](https://pcloudy.medium.com/?source=post_page---byline--8f9ceed936bd---------------------------------------)

[Pcloudy - AI Testing Platform](https://pcloudy.medium.com/?source=post_page---byline--8f9ceed936bd---------------------------------------)

[Listen](https://medium.com/m/signin?actionUrl=https%3A%2F%2Fmedium.com%2Fplans%3Fdimension%3Dpost_audio_button%26postId%3D8f9ceed936bd&operation=register&redirect=https%3A%2F%2Fpcloudy.medium.com%2Funderstanding-xcuitest-framework-your-guide-to-efficient-ios-testing-8f9ceed936bd&source=---header_actions--8f9ceed936bd---------------------post_audio_button------------------)

Press enter or click to view image in full size

![](https://miro.medium.com/v2/resize:fit:700/1*-6jSD5ncpM2Y2lPDC3hXdQ.jpeg)

In today’s era of swift technological progression, effective and efficient testing of iOS applications is vital to ensuring high-quality app development. One such invaluable tool in our testing toolkit is the XCUITest framework developed by Apple. This guide will elucidate what the XCUITest framework is and why it is an ideal choice for iOS testing.

## What is the XCUITest Framework?

The XCUITest framework, launched by Apple in 2015, is a robust automated UI testing tool designed for iOS automation testing. The XCUITest framework operates in conjunction with XCTest, which is a comprehensive test environment integrated into Xcode, Apple’s primary development environment.

This potent combination empowers developers and testers to create automated UI tests for native iOS & macOS applications. Tests are written using either Swift or Objective-C, which are native programming languages to Apple. The XCUITest framework stands as one of the premier [**mobile app testing tools**](https://www.pcloudy.com/), known for its maintainability, resistance to test flakiness, and ability to streamline the continuous integration (CI) process.

## Why Choose XCUITest for iOS Testing?

The mobile market offers an array of testing frameworks, but the XCUITest framework has unique advantages that make it particularly beneficial for [**iOS application testing**](https://www.pcloudy.com/mobile-app-testing-on-multiple-devices/). Here are some of the key reasons to select XCUITest for your iOS app testing needs:

1. Simplicity: Xcode comes pre-loaded with XCUITest, meaning there’s no additional installation required to get started with mobile automation testing.
2. Native Testing Support: The XCUITest framework supports the native iOS languages, Swift and Objective-C. This native support promotes the confident, efficient creation of UI tests, with faster test execution and excellent integration.
3. Promotes Collaboration: XCUITest’s compatibility with native iOS languages ensures developers and testers can collaborate effectively, writing test code in the same language.
4. Fast and Reliable Test Execution: Because XCUITest is specifically designed for UI testing, it assures each unit test and UI test is thorough and error-free. The tests are quick, reliable, and robust.
5. iOS Test Recorder: XCUITest, coupled with XCTest, allows for UI recording using the Xcode IDE. This feature generates test code and records UI interactions, providing a base that testers can adjust and utilize to write effective test cases.
6. Supports Continuous Integration (CI): The XCUITest framework enables seamless integration into the CI process, enabling [**automated app testing**](https://www.pcloudy.com/rapid-automation-testing/). This allows for continuous feedback under actual end-user conditions and on real devices.

## Mastering XCUITest: A Deep Dive into Key Concepts and the XCUITest API

When we consider the practice of automated UI testing for mobile applications, we essentially assess how the user interface reacts to user interactions and compare these test outcomes with our expected results. XCUITest, a robust testing framework developed by Apple, allows us to perform this app test automation efficiently and effectively.

## Unpacking the Core Concepts of XCUITest

The power and proficiency of XCUITest are grounded in two pivotal concepts — XCTest and Accessibility.

1. XCTest: XCTest is a pre-built testing framework in Xcode that enables the creation and execution of UI tests, unit tests, and performance tests for your Xcode projects. Utilizing either Swift or Objective-C languages, XCTest ensures certain conditions are fulfilled during code execution, recording any failures if these conditions aren’t met.
2. Accessibility: [**Accessibility testing**](https://www.pcloudy.com/blogs/mobile-app-accessibility-testing-checklist/) of mobile applications examines the app’s functionality, particularly for people with disabilities. The core functions of the Accessibility feature are used in UI test cases to execute and validate tests.

## A Closer Look at the XCUITest API

To truly harness the power of XCUITest, we need to familiarize ourselves with its API. The XCUITest API offers several classes packed into a single file — XCTest. Here’s an overview of some essential classes you can employ to automate iOS apps:

## XCUITest and Appium: A Comparative Study

Appium provides testing capabilities for both Android and iOS applications and supports multiple programming languages including Java, JavaScript, Python, PHP, and more, whereas XCUITest is specifically tailored for iOS applications and supports Swift and Objective-C, which are native to Apple.

## Limitations of the XCUITest Framework

While XCUITest is a powerful tool, it has a few limitations too:

- While XCUITest provides reliable testing on iOS Simulators and helps avoid flaky tests, the same cannot be ensured when running tests on real devices.
- It’s not ideally suited for smoke or [**regression testing**](https://www.pcloudy.com/a-brief-overview-of-regression-testing/#:~:text=Regression%20testing%20ensures%20the%20proper,is%20not%20feasible%20at%20all.) on real devices.
- XCUITest currently supports only Objective-C and Swift, limiting its language scope.
- It does not provide capabilities for Android automation testing.

## Best Practices for XCUITest

The efficiency of your XCUITest automation script largely depends on your test design, implementation, and analysis. Here are some best practices to make your XCUITest automation suites more efficient and stable:

01. Page Object Model (POM): Page Object Model is a widely used design pattern for test automation that promotes the creation of an object repository to store UI elements. It enhances the maintainability of the tests and reduces code duplication. In the context of XCUITest, you can form Swift classes for each screen in your application, with properties that represent the main elements on the screen.
02. Descriptive Test Names: Adopt the practice of giving descriptive names to your test methods. The test method names should be self-explanatory and indicative of the test’s function. This allows for easy understanding in case of test failures and better maintainability.
03. Use Accessibility Identifiers: Employ Accessibility identifiers to locate UI elements in your application. Accessibility identifiers are user-invisible and don’t need localization, providing a robust way to identify elements.
04. Minimize External Dependencies: Your tests should have minimal dependency on external factors like network conditions or database states. Changes in these conditions may result in test failures.
05. Deal with Flaky Tests: For tests that intermittently fail, identify the cause of flakiness and address it. If it’s not possible, consider flagging these tests as “flaky” and handle them separately.
06. Avoid Hardcoding: Refrain from hard-coding values in your tests. Instead, use constants, variables, or data sources which makes your tests more maintainable.
07. Independent Test Cases: Ensure that each test case is independent of others and does not rely on the state created by other test cases. Independent test cases can be run in isolation, making debugging easier.
08. UI Test Recorder: XCUITest’s built-in UI test recorder is a useful feature that captures information about UI interactions and the internals of your app. Use this feature to record a test interaction and save it as source code.
09. Test After Full Display: Since some views may take time to fully display, always wait for the view to fully display before making any assertions.
10. Specify User Interactions: XCUITest allows you to specify user interactions like a tap(), doubleTap(), and two-finger tap (). With press(), you can specify a duration or both a duration and a dragging target.
11. Run Web Accessibility Tests: Along with UI testing, run web accessibility tests using the UIAccessibility protocol. This will help you to cater to a larger potential customer base, including people with impairments.
12. Create a Framework and Guideline: It’s good practice to create a framework and guideline to aid your team in adding more tests.

By adhering to these best practices and patterns, you can optimize the maintainability and reliability of your XCUITest tests, thereby making your testing process more efficient.

## Troubleshooting Common XCUITest Issues

In the course of working with XCUITest, you may come across some common issues. Here, we aim to provide solutions to these problems:

1. Test Execution Failure: At times, XCUITest may fail to execute tests due to issues like unrecognized selectors or unexpected nil values. This can be mitigated by ensuring that your test methods do not contain any parameters and return void.
2. Interacting with System Alerts: XCUITest may have trouble interacting with system alerts, causing test cases to fail. To handle these alerts, use the addUIInterruptionMonitor(withDescription: handler:) method to set up handlers for expected system alerts.
3. Accessibility Identifiers not Set: XCUITest may fail to find elements if their accessibility identifiers are not set. Ensure that all UI elements that need to be interacted with have accessibility identifiers.

## Advanced XCUITest Techniques

XCUITest not only allows for basic UI testing but also enables users to handle complex testing scenarios. Here are a few examples:

1. Testing Asynchronous Behavior: Testing asynchronous operations like network requests or timers can be tricky. XCUITest provides XCTestExpectation and the wait(for timeout:) function that allows for testing asynchronous code by pausing the test execution until a condition is met or a timeout occurs.
2. Handling System Interruptions: XCUITest can handle system interruptions like alerts and notifications using the addUIInterruptionMonitor(withDescription: handler:) function. This method allows setting up handlers that can dismiss or interact with these alerts, enabling the test to continue.
3. Screen Interactions: Complex user interactions such as swipes, pinch, drag, or rotate gestures can be simulated using XCUIElement‘s various functions like swipeLeft(), swipe-right (), pinch(with scale: velocity:), and more.

By leveraging these advanced techniques, you can make your tests more robust and simulate a wide variety of user interactions, ensuring thorough test coverage.
